1. No poverty.summary: No supporting evidence was found to determine the proportion of the population living below the international poverty line. The context mentions that the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC) is one of the poorest countries in the world with a human development index of 0.29 in 2011 and a GDP of USD 220 per capita (Document: 28+May+2020+Verification+Report_Congo+project+Forestry_Clean.pdf, Page: 26). However, it does not provide specific data on the proportion of the population living below the international poverty line. The evidence is qualitative and does not directly measure the indicator variable.

: k. Socio- ‐economic history and current situation | before and only VERs will be issued.: DRC  remains  one  of  the  poorest  countries  in  the  world  with  a  human  development  index  of  0.29  in  2011  and  a  very  high  population’s  growth  rate  and  GDP  of  USD  220  per  capita.  The  verification  team  observed that forests are an important source of local  livelihoods  and  economy  for  the  forest  dependent  people  throughout  the  country.  Forests  provide  fuelwood,  fruits,  and  other  food  and  household  products.  The  vast  majority  of  wood  harvested  in  the  region  is  used  for  wood  fuel,  either  directly  or  processed  as  charcoal.    As  DRC  develops  and  urbanizes,  fuel  wood collection will continue to be a threat for forests  of the region, especially around urban areas.  In much of the region, industrial logging concessions  are  granted  for  the  extraction  of  valuable  timber.  Logging  contributes  to  government  revenues  and  rural  employment,  but  unsustainable  practices  can  causes  forest  degradation  and  detriment  to  rural  livelihoods.  Moreover,  unsustainable  logging  opens  forests  to  poaching,  illegal  wildlife  trade,  and  sometimes  further  forest  clearing.  The  verification  team  found  that  some  of  the  plantations  are  very  unsafe to visit due to security reasons due to rebels.  Illegal logging in the Congo Basin Forest is difficult to  estimate  but  suspected  to  be  widespread.  In  the  basin,  as  in  the  rest  of  the  world,  illegal  logging  causes  forest  degradation,  impacts  rural  communities,  and  can  decrease  tax  revenues.  Reforestation  produces  goods  of  high  interest  for  communities (firewood, timber, etc) which could result  in  illegal  logging  and  thus  leads  conflicts  in  the  community.  But  the  communities  themselves  have  a  conflict  management  in  place,  hence  this  can  be  avoided.  Based  on  the  opinion  of  local  technical  expert  and  interview  with  PP,  the  verification  team  has  confirmed  that  there  is  no  illegal  planation  or  illegal occupation of armed forces.

j. Land--‐use history and current situation of the 
project area 
The verification team reviewed the land history of 
North Kivu and confirmed that human history in the 
region is defined by their relationship with forests and 
woodlands.
Soils are usually no fertile and plantations 
are often located in low fertile soils.
Most of the

implemented in Democratic Republic of Congo, in the five territories i.e.,

Socio- ‐economic history and current situation | before and only VERs will be issued.: DRC  remains  one  of  the  poorest  countries  in  the  world  with  a  human  development  index  of  0.29  in  2011  and  a  very  high  population’s  growth  rate  and  GDP  of  USD  220  per  capita.  The  verification  team  observed that forests are an important source of local  livelihoods  and  economy  for  the  forest  dependent  people  throughout  the  country.  Forests  provide  fuelwood,  fruits,  and  other  food  and  household  products.  The  vast  majority  of  wood  harvested  in  the  region  is  used  for  wood  fuel,  either  directly  or  processed  as  charcoal.    As  DRC  develops  and  urbanizes,  fuel  wood collection will continue to be a threat for forests  of the region, especially around urban areas.  In much of the region, industrial logging concessions  are  granted  for  the  extraction  of  valuable  timber.  Logging  contributes  to  government  revenues  and  rural  employment,  but  unsustainable  practices  can  causes  forest  degradation  and  detriment  to  rural  livelihoods.  Moreover,  unsustainable  logging  opens  forests  to  poaching,  illegal  wildlife  trade,  and  sometimes  further  forest  clearing.  The  verification  team  found  that  some  of  the  plantations  are  very  unsafe to visit due to security reasons due to rebels.  Illegal logging in the Congo Basin Forest is difficult to  estimate  but  suspected  to  be  widespread.  In  the  basin,  as  in  the  rest  of  the  world,  illegal  logging  causes  forest  degradation,  impacts  rural  communities,  and  can  decrease  tax  revenues.  Reforestation  produces  goods  of  high  interest  for  communities (firewood, timber, etc) which could result  in  illegal  logging  and  thus  leads  conflicts  in  the  community.  But  the  communities  themselves  have  a  conflict  management  in  place,  hence  this  can  be  avoided.  Based  on  the  opinion  of  local  technical  expert  and  interview  with  PP,  the  verification  team  has  confirmed  that  there  is  no  illegal  planation  or  illegal occupation of armed forces.

Zero hunger.summary: No supporting evidence was found in the provided context to calculate the percentage change in Soil Organic Carbon (SOC) stock in agricultural lands within the project area during the reporting period. The context mentions that the SOC calculation was carried out using the Gold Standard 'A/R Soil Carbon Tool' but does not provide the actual values or percentage changes. The total area included for SOC calculation is mentioned as 2764.808 ha, but this information is not sufficient to calculate the percentage change in SOC stock. The baseline SOC stock level, SOC stock level after implementation, and duration of the project activity are also not provided. Therefore, the evidence does not satisfy the indicator's measurement requirement.

There  is  no  detailed  cartographic  information  available that allows us to discriminate between types of crops, grassland  (pastures) and trees.   For  agriculture  and  grassland  only  one  stratum,  namely  the  more  56  conservative one (grassland), in baseline is assigned, hence a conservative  carbon stock will be used.

The  verification  team  has  reviewed  the  contractual  terms/11/  between  the  project participants with planters, associations. Hence  accepted the same.: The  project  area  is  located  in  the  province  of  North  Kivu,  around  the  Virunga  National  Park  in  eastern  Democratic  Republic  of  Congo  (DRC).  The  primary  zone  of  intervention  is  the  charcoal  supply  basin,  around Goma (Masisi, Rutshuru and Nyiragongo); the  secondary  zone  comprises  the  territories  of  Lubero,  Beni.  Till  2016,  the  planting  area  is  around  10,000  hectares.  The  eligible  planting  area  is  1,878    hectares.  The  verification  team  has  visited  the  19  plantations and accepted the same.

Good health and well being.summary: The evidence from the survey conducted with 107 randomly selected growers (planters) confirms that no biomass is burned at the site, and none of the surveyed growers indicated burning the biomass that is part of the project activity (Document: CCIPL+1586_Final+Performance+Certification+Report+v04-1_clean.pdf, Page: 36). The baseline area under open burning of biomass/crop residue before the implementation of sustainable land management practices is 10% of the total area (Document: 28+May+2020+Verification+Report_Congo+project+Forestry_Clean.pdf, Page: 39). The percentage change in land area under open burning of biomass/crop residue after the implementation of sustainable land management practices is 100% decrease (Document: CCIPL+1586_Final+Performance+Certification+Report+v04-1_clean.pdf, Page: 36). The evidence directly measures the indicator variable, and the explicit quantitative data supports the indicator.

The  verification  team  visited  19  plantations  and  as  per  acceptance  sampling  accepted  the  inventory  details.  The  verification  team  accepted  the  value  of  steam  volume, biomass expansion factor, root to shoot ratio,  form factor using stem analysis, wood density for the  species,  and  IPCC  default  values  by  reviewing  the  relevant sources.  he  verification  team  reviewed  the  Carbon  fixation  excel  sheets  and  observed  that  if  there  was  any  sanitary  felling,  that  would  have  been  taken  into  account  during  inventory.  Carbon  fixation  calculation  is  based  on  the  inventory  so  any  felling  would  have  been  included  in  the  final  calculations.  The  planters  are  well  aware  of  the  harvesting  plan  for  their  plantations. Based on the interview with planters and  onsite  visit,  there  is  no  risk  of  overcutting  of  trees.  Hence the verification team closed the issue   he  verification  team  has  also  confirmed  that  the  process of the forest inventory is clearly documented  and can be easily replicated.   The  verification  team  observed  that  after  the  inventory,  the  internal  quality  control  team,  independent  of  the  inventory  team,  randomly  inventoried some of the plantations and no significant  errors  were  found.  Moreover,  the  verification  team  also  checked  the  inventory  results  with  what  was  obtained during the DOE plantation visits and results  are reasonable. Hence quality control is robust.

E In  section  B1.1  of  the  monitoring  report,  responses  on  the  FARs  were  formulated.  The  same  responses have been added here.   coMakala  project  partners  are  prohibited  from  using  fertilizers  and  pesticides  in  reforestation  activities under the project without consulting WWF's advice. This was stressed during the periodical  awareness  raising  campaigns  (see  document  ’17.10.2018.Normes  techniques  de_plantation’,  ‘Formation_protection_plantations_  juillet2018’).  In  addition,  a  survey  carried  out  on    with  150  randomly selected planters further confirmed that none of the planters use synthetic fertilisers or  soil  amendments  on  their  plantations  (see  document  ‘20220927_Survey  with  planters_MP4’).  Documentation  provided  by  project  developer.  This  monitoring  period  fewer  trainings  than  anticipated took place. This can be explained by several reasons, including the Ebola outbreak in  2018-2020 in the North Kivu region , the global COVID-19 pandemic (2020 to 2022), both of which  involved measures including travel and gathering restrictions. This has affected the implementation  of the project and monitoring activities. The change of PP which included a transition period and  takeover of operations from WWF to UGP also posed some challenges on the follow-up. However,  now the new structure is fully completed and in place. The PP will ensure to continue inform planters  about the use of fertilizers and pesticides as well in future through awareness raising campaigns.: Documentation provided by project developer

SECTION A. DESCRIPTION OF PROJECT 
A.1.
General description of project  
 
Since more than two decades, the World Wide Fund for Nature (WWF) promotes various 
activities in North Kivu in order to protect the Virunga Natural Park (ViNP) and alleviate 
poverty of the surrounding communities.
One of these activities, and probably the most 
important, is the EcoMakala1 project, which consist in the improvement of the charcoal 
sector.

E In  section  B1.1  of  the  monitoring  report,  responses  on  the  FARs  were  formulated.  The  same  responses have been added here.   coMakala  project  partners  are  prohibited  from  using  fertilizers  and  pesticides  in  reforestation  activities under the project without consulting WWF's advice. This was stressed during the periodical  awareness  raising  campaigns  (see  document  ’17.10.2018.Normes  techniques  de_plantation’,  ‘Formation_protection_plantations_  juillet2018’).  In  addition,  a  survey  carried  out  on    with  150  randomly selected planters further confirmed that none of the planters use synthetic fertilisers or  soil  amendments  on  their  plantations  (see  document  ‘20220927_Survey  with  planters_MP4’).  Documentation  provided  by  project  developer.  This  monitoring  period  fewer  trainings  than  anticipated took place. This can be explained by several reasons, including the Ebola outbreak in  2018-2020 in the North Kivu region , the global COVID-19 pandemic (2020 to 2022), both of which  involved measures including travel and gathering restrictions. This has affected the implementation  of the project and monitoring activities. The change of PP which included a transition period and  takeover of operations from WWF to UGP also posed some challenges on the follow-up. However,  now the new structure is fully completed and in place. The PP will ensure to continue inform planters  about the use of fertilizers and pesticides as well in future through awareness raising campaigns.: H   F VVB,  based  on  the  justification  and  supporting  documents,  confirms  that  EcoMakala  project  partners  are  prohibited  from  using  fertilizers  and  pesticides  in  reforestation  activities  under  the  project  without  consulting  WWF's  advice.  Further  through  the  assessment  of  “20220927_Survey  with  planters_MP4”,  VVB  confirms  that  a  comprehensive  survey  was  conducted,  involving  150  randomly  selected  planters.  The  survey  results  confirm  that  none  of  the  planters  reported  using  synthetic fertilizers or soil amendments on their plantations. PD assures that they will continue to  educate and inform planters about the importance of avoiding the use of fertilizers and pesticides.  ence, VVB confirms the EcoMakala project's commitment to sustainable practices by prohibiting  the use of fertilizers and pesticides in reforestation activities. The survey results further support the  adherence of planters to these guidelines, and PD's commitment to ongoing education ensures that  sustainable practices will continue to be upheld throughout the project's duration. Means of validation  DR, OSV: Conclusion | : V As per review of GS MR/02/, the training sessions were provided to the growers  for ensuring the sustainable forest management including the installation of  nurseries,  harvesting  and  maintenance  of  the  plantation.  Furthermore,  the  training sessions were also aimed towards raising the awareness of growers  to keep the plantations well maintained and clean during the dry season and  to  create  5-meter  firewalls  around  the  plantations,  particularly  for  the  plantations which are adjacent to the bush.   There were  around  15 workshops/trainings held by the year  2021  whereas  during this monitoring period 2 training sessions were conducted.   Furthermore, during this monitoring period fewer trainings than the anticipated  took place due to several reasons such as Ebola outbreak in  2018-2020 in  North Kivu region, the global COVID-19 pandemic (2020-2022), both of which  included the travelling and gathering restrictions. Additionally, change of PD  which included a transition period and takeover of operations from WWF to  UGP further resulted in the follow-up challenges.  VB, based on the  training planners/28/, confirms that  1 training session for  planters  in  2018  and  1  sensibilization  session  for  planters  in  2019  were  provided  to  the  growers  for  ensuring  the  sustainable  forest  management  including  the  installation  of  nurseries,  harvesting  and  maintenance  of  the  plantation.

4. Quality education.summary: The evidence from the document '28+May+2020+Verification+Report_Congo+project+Forestry_Clean.pdf' on page 30-31 and 'CCIPL+1586_Final+Performance+Certification+Report+v04-1_clean.pdf' on page 21, 42, mentions that employees have access to relevant skills development opportunities through training. However, the evidence does not provide explicit quantitative data on the number of training hours provided for employees, disaggregated per gender. The mention of 'on-field training provided during establishment and maintenance activities' and 'training records show a positive impact on quality of employment' is qualitative and does not directly measure the indicator variable. The proportion of women in nurseries (9.3%) and the number of associations managed by women (11) is also qualitative and does not directly measure the indicator variable. Therefore, the evidence does not satisfy the indicator's measurement requirement.

activity (e.g. burning of remaining biomass after harvest of trees).

The verification team's finding that all Eucalyptus plantations are away from water bodies with a minimum distance of 20 meters (28+May+2020+Verification+Report_Congo+project+Forestry_Clean.pdf, Page: 34) is also qualitative and does not directly measure the indicator variable. Therefore, the evidence does not satisfy the indicator's measurement requirement.

Clean water and sanitation.summary: The evidence from the verification report (Document: 28+May+2020+Verification+Report_Congo+project+Forestry_Clean.pdf, Page: 34) shows that all 19 Eucalyptus plantations visited were at least 20 meters away from water bodies, as required. This qualitative evidence (Score = 1) demonstrates the project's measures to maintain a safe distance between plantations and water bodies, but it does not directly measure the water retention capacity. Additionally, the monitoring report (Document: Monitoring-Report_PerfCert2_GS5618_Ecomakala+Virunga+Reforestation+project_v4.1_clean.pdf, Page: 52) mentions that growers identify water sources at the time of signing the contract, which is a qualitative indicator of the project's approach to water management (Score = 1). However, neither report provides explicit quantitative data that directly measures the water retention capacity.

The forest objective is production of  sustainable  charcoal,  which  is  the  main  source  of  energy  in  the  region.  The  project  also  increases  the  forest plantation area and helps in technology transfer  to other community plantations.

The EcoMakala Virunga Reforestation project concerns the first activity, under the LUF 
scope.
The last two activities (EcoMakala Virunga Energy project) is being certified as a 
GS energy VER project.
The reforestation project consists in plantation of fast growing trees over deforested 
lands (at least deforested from 1997, i.e. at least 10 years before project start).

The  activities  started at the end of 2007 and is aimed towards planting of up to 12,000 ha  eligible for GS certification under 2025. By the end of 2018, around 10,000 ha  have already planted out of which around 3,569 ha are eligible in accordance  with requirements of Gold Standard. Although the total number of employees is mentioned (33), the local minimum wage is not explicitly stated, and the percentage of employees earning above the local minimum wage is mentioned as 100%, but this is based on an assumption that all employees earn above the local minimum wage. The evidence is qualitative and does not contain explicit quantitative data that directly measures the indicator.

The mention of 7 foresters and 4 service providers employed (CCIPL+1586_Final+Performance+Certification+Report+v04-1_clean.pdf, Page: 22) is a quantitative data point, but it does not directly measure income generation from other activities, such as ecotourism. Therefore, the evidence partially satisfies the measurement requirement, but not fully.

8. Decent work and economic growth.summary: The provided evidence does not directly measure the average hourly earnings of employees by sex, age, occupation, and persons with disabilities. Although it mentions that the project activity created jobs with salaries better than the average local/sector wage level, this information is qualitative and does not satisfy the measurement requirement. The verification team observed that the jobs created do not present any significant negative impact in quantitative employment, but this is also qualitative and does not provide explicit quantitative data. The proportion of women in nurseries is 9.3% and 7 associations are managed by women, but these statistics do not measure average hourly earnings. The average DBH of tree in sample plot of stratum 5-EM is 8.93 cm, which is unrelated to the indicator variable. The number of service providers due to the project activity with salaries/wages at par or better than the average local/sector wage level is 4, but this is also qualitative and does not provide explicit quantitative data. The legal working age in the host country is 18 years old, and the verification team has confirmed that the requirements of the ILO Fundamental Conventions are being followed, but these details are unrelated to the indicator variable. The verification team has reviewed the labour code/Congolese labour law/16/ and the law prohibits discrimination in employment and occupation based on race, gender, language, or social status, including disability, but this is qualitative and does not provide explicit quantitative data.

12. Responsible consumption and producti.summary: The evidence does not directly measure the average reduction in synthetic fertilizer consumption per ha. Although the context mentions that growers don't use synthetic fertilizers, organic manure, or return of residues or cover crops fixing nitrogen on their plantations (Document: Monitoring-Report_PerfCert2_GS5618_Ecomakala+Virunga+Reforestation+project_v4.1_clean.pdf, Page: 19), no quantitative data is provided to calculate the average reduction. The discount factor for the practice of agroforestry / intercropping has been calculated per previous usage of the plantation: (i) agriculture: 79.49%; and (ii) grassland: 81.34% (Document: Monitoring-Report_PerfCert2_GS5618_Ecomakala+Virunga+Reforestation+project_v4.1_clean.pdf, Page: 38), but this is not directly related to the indicator variable. Therefore, the evidence is treated as qualitative (Score = 1).

E In  section  B1.1  of  the  monitoring  report,  responses  on  the  FARs  were  formulated.  The  same  responses have been added here.   coMakala  project  partners  are  prohibited  from  using  fertilizers  and  pesticides  in  reforestation  activities under the project without consulting WWF's advice. This was stressed during the periodical  awareness  raising  campaigns  (see  document  ’17.10.2018.Normes  techniques  de_plantation’,  ‘Formation_protection_plantations_  juillet2018’).  In  addition,  a  survey  carried  out  on    with  150  randomly selected planters further confirmed that none of the planters use synthetic fertilisers or  soil  amendments  on  their  plantations  (see  document  ‘20220927_Survey  with  planters_MP4’).  Documentation  provided  by  project  developer.  This  monitoring  period  fewer  trainings  than  anticipated took place. This can be explained by several reasons, including the Ebola outbreak in  2018-2020 in the North Kivu region , the global COVID-19 pandemic (2020 to 2022), both of which  involved measures including travel and gathering restrictions. This has affected the implementation  of the project and monitoring activities. The change of PP which included a transition period and  takeover of operations from WWF to UGP also posed some challenges on the follow-up. However,  now the new structure is fully completed and in place. The PP will ensure to continue inform planters  about the use of fertilizers and pesticides as well in future through awareness raising campaigns.: Formation_protection_plantations_juillet2018’  -  20220927_Survey with planters_MP4  -

Also displacement of grazing into the PNVRi or other wood land  should be assessed given the intensity of pastoralism in the project zone.   The response from PP stated that PP will ask each growers at the time of signing the contract amendment if  there has been or is expected in the future any shift of agricultural or livestock activities which might cause  leakage. If survey responses reveal that significant leakage occurs (contrary to what was expected ex-ante  as per template 5.6), leakage will be calculated following the guidance indicated on pages 34/35 of the A/R  requirements and be deducted in the first year (t=1). In regards to risks caused by animals from the PNVRi  (like elephants and gorillas), the same is minimal and not considered to be significant. All plantations have to  have a certain security margin to the park, i.e. have to be located at least 200 meters from the national park’s  borders. In a few areas of the park where some risk of migrating animals to outside of the park have been  identified, an electric fence has been built. It should be further noted that neither gorillas nor elephants feed  with any of the species included in the project. The following table illustrates some of the environmental and socio-economic benefits 
of the project3:  
 
Environmental benefits  
Biodiversity 
The deforestation pressure on the Virunga Park is reduced by the 
supply of alternative wood energy from plantations outside the park.
The forest plantations promote recolonization of the buffer zone of the

Environmental benefits: Soil & water | : In terms of soil, the forest plantations lead to stabilization of soils and  fight  against  erosion,  soil  restauration  in  case  of  degraded  soils  and  thus, protection of watershed. Besides, the soil fertility is improved - when  planting  leguminous  species  which  enrich  nitrogen.  In  addition  plantation  forests  have  a  "filter"  role  in  the  water  cycle,  helping  to  improve  the  quality  of  stormwater  runoff,  reducing  surface  runoff,  increased infiltration into the soil and evapotranspiration.

Environmental benefits: Biodiversity | : The  deforestation  pressure  on  the  Virunga  Park  is  reduced  by  the  supply of alternative wood energy from plantations outside the park.   The forest plantations promote recolonization of the buffer zone of the  PNVI  by  animal  biodiversity  and  vegetal  and  an  indirect  effect  of  limiting illegal logging and poaching in the park.

On  both  sides  of  permanent  or  temporary  water  bodies  (lakes,  streams,  rivers,  wetlands,  etc.)  riparian  buffer  zones  of  15  meters  shall  be  implemented  on  each  site.  In  these  riparian  buffer  zones:  (a) only native tree species3 may be planted, AND  (b) invasive species4 shall be removed, AND  (c) all existing vegetation shall be kept, AND  (d) no  timber  harvesting  activities  shall  take  place,  AND  (e) no use of fertilizer or chemical pesticides.  onclusion: | plantation follows the  land  contours  by  designing  the  rip  lines  accordingly.  For  prevention  of  compacting,  the  soil  is  ripped  up  during  planting  and  also  sheep  grazing is done in a controlled way. Hence the project  fulfills the GS requirements as relevant to the criteria  for the areas under Performance certification.: This  requirement  is  not  relevant  since  this  project  area  is  managed  by  small  holders.  However  eucalyptus trees are planted no less than 20 m away  from  the  water  bodies.  The  verification  team  visited  one  plantation  and  found  the  same.  The  verification  team  has  reviewed  the  shapefiles/13/  and  based  on  the  onsite  visit,  has  identified  the  water  bodies.  The  verification  team  has  also  observed  that  there  is  no   tree species  growth  at  the  conservation  area.  All  the  vegetation  is  kept  as  it  is.  No  timber  activities  took  place.

One of the objectives of these plantations is the sustainable production of charcoal, 
which is the main source of energy in the region.
In fact, almost 100% of energy 
requirements in North-Kivu are provided by biomass, either as firewood or charcoal.
Biomass comes mainly from non-sustainable sources and even illegal as from the PNVi, 
to such a point that today, most of forest cover around urban zones of the region has 
disappeared.

15. Life on land.summary: The evidence provided for the indicator 'Forest areas managed sustainably for forest products including sustainable produced fuelwood' is mostly qualitative. The total area of forest land under sustainable management practices is mentioned as 3,154 ha, but this is not directly measured in the context of the indicator. The eligible planting area is 1,878 hectares, but this is also not directly related to the indicator. The percentage of forest areas certified under recognized sustainable forest management standards is not explicitly stated. The types of forest management practices being implemented are mentioned, but this is qualitative evidence. The key factors influencing the adoption of sustainable forest management practices are also mentioned, but this is qualitative evidence. The average annual rate of increase in the area of forest land under sustainable management practices is not calculable due to lack of data. Therefore, the score is 0.

What is the change in above ground bioma.content: C 2  -  L ID  Section no.  Date: 14/10/2018: The PD would like to point out that leakage as per GS A/R requirements, section 5.6. is defined as ‘shift of  activities  from  the  inside  of  a  project  area  to  the  outside  of  a  project  area’.  Forest  fire  (if  any)  or  cutting  of  trees occur inside of the project area, hence cannot be classified as leakage.   The  decrease  of  biomass  due  to  forest  fire  and  cutting  of  trees  is  reflected  in  the  randomly  selected,  inventoried  sample  plots.  In  case  that  a  forest  fire  has  destroyed  all  or  a  part  of  the  biomass  located  on  a  sample  plot,  no  or  reduced  tree  biomass  will  be  measured  which  again  has  a  negative  impact  on  the  total  biomass in the respective strata and will finally result in less emission reduction credits. The same happens in  case that trees have been cut and their biomass cannot be accounted for in the ER calculation anymore. The  auditor during his on-site mission visited 2 plantations, which were slightly affected by forest fire and 3 more  plantations  on  which  a  considerable  number  of  trees  were  cut.  Since  the  inventory  only  measures  that  biomass which is present at the time of measurements, i.e. considers any forest fire or harvest/cut which had  happened before and extrapolates the  calculated  average  amount  of  biomass  to  the  total  population  in  the  strata.  Any  forest  fires  or  harvest/cut  are  conservatively  taken  into  account  in  the  calculation  of  CO2  certificates.  The  auditor  by  carrying  out  the  measurements  in  randomly  selected  plantations  was  able  to  corroborate that the biomass stock had been correctly reflected in the inventories, also for those plantations  which were slightly affected by forest fires. In case of two Acacia plantations the auditor visited, no biomass  could  be  found  on  the  sample  plot  anymore.  This  cut  happened  after  the  inventories  had  been  conducted  which  explains  why  the  inventories  still  state  a  positive  biomass  stock.  The  PD  decided  to  amend  the  biomass stock of these 2 plantations to zero (see response in CAR 1).

Date: 14/10/2018: The PD would like to point out that leakage as per GS A/R requirements, section 5.6. is defined as ‘shift of  activities  from  the  inside  of  a  project  area  to  the  outside  of  a  project  area’.  Forest  fire  (if  any)  or  cutting  of  trees occur inside of the project area, hence cannot be classified as leakage.   The  decrease  of  biomass  due  to  forest  fire  and  cutting  of  trees  is  reflected  in  the  randomly  selected,  inventoried  sample  plots.  In  case  that  a  forest  fire  has  destroyed  all  or  a  part  of  the  biomass  located  on  a  sample  plot,  no  or  reduced  tree  biomass  will  be  measured  which  again  has  a  negative  impact  on  the  total  biomass in the respective strata and will finally result in less emission reduction credits. The same happens in  case that trees have been cut and their biomass cannot be accounted for in the ER calculation anymore. The  auditor during his on-site mission visited 2 plantations, which were slightly affected by forest fire and 3 more  plantations  on  which  a  considerable  number  of  trees  were  cut.  Since  the  inventory  only  measures  that  biomass which is present at the time of measurements, i.e. considers any forest fire or harvest/cut which had  happened before and extrapolates the  calculated  average  amount  of  biomass  to  the  total  population  in  the  strata.  Any  forest  fires  or  harvest/cut  are  conservatively  taken  into  account  in  the  calculation  of  CO2  certificates.  The  auditor  by  carrying  out  the  measurements  in  randomly  selected  plantations  was  able  to  corroborate that the biomass stock had been correctly reflected in the inventories, also for those plantations  which were slightly affected by forest fires. In case of two Acacia plantations the auditor visited, no biomass  could  be  found  on  the  sample  plot  anymore.  This  cut  happened  after  the  inventories  had  been  conducted  which  explains  why  the  inventories  still  state  a  positive  biomass  stock.  The  PD  decided  to  amend  the  biomass stock of these 2 plantations to zero (see response in CAR 1).

What is the extent of forest restoration.content: : Additional  comment | 30%; -single minimum land area value of 0.5 ha and a single minimum  tree height value of 3 meters.  Eucalyptus  plantations  within  20  meters  distance  from  watercourses/- sources are considered as non-eligible.  During  the  forest  inventory,  the  surfaces  of  the  sampled  plantations  are  remeasured and is being compared with the initially measured surface area.  Based  on the difference, a correction  factor  for each plantation has been  calculated.    In  case  that  the  average  correction  factor  for  the  respective  stratum  has  been  bigger  than  1,  i.e.  surface  area  measured  during  the  inventories  is  bigger  than  the  initial  surface  measurement,  the  lower  initially measured surface area has been maintained for conservatism. In  case that the correction factor of the respective stratum has been smaller  than 1, the initially measured surface area has been reduced accordingly  by  the  correction  factor.  The  surface  areas  presented  here  are  the  ones       without the correction factor.: A new species was added to the project boundary, hence for this  monitoring period, a total of 7 strata have been defined according to the  species involved in the project activity:  Eucalyptus Saligna, Grevillea  Robusta, Acacia Mearnsii, Cedrela Odorata, Senna Siamea, Eucalyptus  Maidenii and Acrocarpus fraxinifolius. However, there was decided to  exclude stratum 4 and 7 and to not claim any carbon credits for the 2nd  performance certification due to the low sample size number. These strata  make up only 2.4% and 0.3% of the total eligible area.

The  verification  team  has  reviewed  the  contractual  terms/11/  between  the  project participants with planters, associations. Hence  accepted the same.: The  project  promotes  activities  for  the  protection  of  the  forests  of  Virunga  National  Park  and  alleviate  poverty  of  the  eligible  communities  (planters)  in  the  project boundary. The forest objective is production of  sustainable  charcoal,  which  is  the  main  source  of  energy  in  the  region.  The  project  also  increases  the  forest plantation area and helps in technology transfer  to other community plantations.

Date: 14/10/2018: ( Corresponding  to  the  FAR9  raised  by  GS  on  the  document  [File  FARs  Ecomakala_Jul11_final],  GS  stated  that Project areas are composed by agricultural and grazing/pasture lands, without forests at least 10 years  prior to project start date (20150928 Rapport_Detude_eligibilite_Ecomakala_V6 and shape files ‘Shapefiles –  Planting area’). Also in the mitigation measures, the proximity of the planting zones with PNVRi, risk elephant  and gorillas damages was not considered. Also displacement of grazing into the PNVRi or other wood land  should be assessed given the intensity of pastoralism in the project zone.   The response from PP stated that PP will ask each growers at the time of signing the contract amendment if  there has been or is expected in the future any shift of agricultural or livestock activities which might cause  leakage. If survey responses reveal that significant leakage occurs (contrary to what was expected ex-ante  as per template 5.6), leakage will be calculated following the guidance indicated on pages 34/35 of the A/R  requirements and be deducted in the first year (t=1). In regards to risks caused by animals from the PNVRi  (like elephants and gorillas), the same is minimal and not considered to be significant. All plantations have to  have a certain security margin to the park, i.e. have to be located at least 200 meters from the national park’s  borders. In a few areas of the park where some risk of migrating animals to outside of the park have been  identified, an electric fence has been built. It should be further noted that neither gorillas nor elephants feed  with any of the species included in the project.   The  review  by  GS  stated  that  :  Based  on  the  ONfi  report  and  further  assessment  of  the  expansion  of  agrosilvopastural land. We can keep this as an observation to be analyze after the interviews assessment of  the  growers.  The  interviews  questionnaire  should  be  provided  as  a  supporting  document  as  well  with  the  sample included in the assessment.   The  interview  questionnaire  as  well  as  some  sample  copies  of  interviews  carried  out  with  growers  will  be  available to the DOE during the initial certification.   During  the  site  visit,  The  questionnaire  of  the  survey  has  been  added  with  the  question  related  to  the  expectation of the growers to practice agricultural or livestock activities in the future and/or if they displaced  the  agricultural  and  livestock  activities  in  other  localities/lands  due  to  reforestation  activity.  A  sample  of  5  survey have been filled and made available to DOE during the site visit.   Netherthless,  this  sample  is  not  significative  to  decide  at  this  stage  if  there  is  leakage  through  agricultural  activities or not.   FAR:  Hence,  the  PP  is  encouraged  to  continue  the  survey,  and  once  the  the  majority  of  responses  completed, it will reveal if significant leakage occurs so then a recalculation of leakage would be necessary.  The Forward Action Request shall be considered during the “Performance Assessment”.   Note: this FAR is carried over from initial certification and GS review.)

During the certification report, the site visit was undertaken in order to check the project compliance, not only  to ensure that the planting of trees occur on land that does not meet the definition of forest at planting start;  but  also  to  cross  check  the  proximity  of  the  planting  to  the  water  bodies.  Validation  team  visited  the  plantations  in  the  primary  zone  of  intervention  which  is  the  charcoal  supply  basin  around  Goma  in  the  3  Territoires: Nyiragongo, Masisi, Rutshuru, among 5 territories in the project area.   The territory of Lubero and beni  being  the  secondary  planting  area  (as  per  the  project  description).  Lubero  plantations are the most exposed to fire which is a main driver of none permanence of Co2 certificate claims.  Also the territories of Lubero and Beni are mining areas that can significantly impact the project activities.   In order to ensure the representativeness of the sampling parcels in the different planting territories, all the 5  territories  of  North-Kivu  province,  namely  Nyiragongo,  Masisi,  Rutshuru,  Lubero  and  Beni  shall  be  represented on the plantations assessment during the performance certification audit   Note: this FAR is carried over from initial certification and GS review.)

On  both  sides  of  permanent  or  temporary  water  bodies  (lakes,  streams,  rivers,  wetlands,  etc.)  riparian  buffer  zones  of  15  meters  shall  be  implemented  on  each  site.  In  these  riparian  buffer  zones:  (a) only native tree species3 may be planted, AND  (b) invasive species4 shall be removed, AND  (c) all existing vegetation shall be kept, AND  (d) no  timber  harvesting  activities  shall  take  place,  AND  (e) no use of fertilizer or chemical pesticides.  onclusion: | plantation follows the  land  contours  by  designing  the  rip  lines  accordingly.  For  prevention  of  compacting,  the  soil  is  ripped  up  during  planting  and  also  sheep  grazing is done in a controlled way. Hence the project  fulfills the GS requirements as relevant to the criteria  for the areas under Performance certification.: This  requirement  is  not  relevant  since  this  project  area  is  managed  by  small  holders. Date: 14/10/2018: ( Corresponding  to  the  FAR9  raised  by  GS  on  the  document  [File  FARs  Ecomakala_Jul11_final],  GS  stated  that Project areas are composed by agricultural and grazing/pasture lands, without forests at least 10 years  prior to project start date (20150928 Rapport_Detude_eligibilite_Ecomakala_V6 and shape files ‘Shapefiles –  Planting area’). Also in the mitigation measures, the proximity of the planting zones with PNVRi, risk elephant  and gorillas damages was not considered. Also displacement of grazing into the PNVRi or other wood land  should be assessed given the intensity of pastoralism in the project zone.   The response from PP stated that PP will ask each growers at the time of signing the contract amendment if  there has been or is expected in the future any shift of agricultural or livestock activities which might cause  leakage. If survey responses reveal that significant leakage occurs (contrary to what was expected ex-ante  as per template 5.6), leakage will be calculated following the guidance indicated on pages 34/35 of the A/R  requirements and be deducted in the first year (t=1). In regards to risks caused by animals from the PNVRi  (like elephants and gorillas), the same is minimal and not considered to be significant. All plantations have to  have a certain security margin to the park, i.e. have to be located at least 200 meters from the national park’s  borders. In a few areas of the park where some risk of migrating animals to outside of the park have been  identified, an electric fence has been built. It should be further noted that neither gorillas nor elephants feed  with any of the species included in the project.   The  review  by  GS  stated  that  :  Based  on  the  ONfi  report  and  further  assessment  of  the  expansion  of  agrosilvopastural land. We can keep this as an observation to be analyze after the interviews assessment of  the  growers.  The  interviews  questionnaire  should  be  provided  as  a  supporting  document  as  well  with  the  sample included in the assessment.   The  interview  questionnaire  as  well  as  some  sample  copies  of  interviews  carried  out  with  growers  will  be  available to the DOE during the initial certification.   During  the  site  visit,  The  questionnaire  of  the  survey  has  been  added  with  the  question  related  to  the  expectation of the growers to practice agricultural or livestock activities in the future and/or if they displaced  the  agricultural  and  livestock  activities  in  other  localities/lands  due  to  reforestation  activity.  A  sample  of  5  survey have been filled and made available to DOE during the site visit.   Netherthless,  this  sample  is  not  significative  to  decide  at  this  stage  if  there  is  leakage  through  agricultural  activities or not.   FAR:  Hence,  the  PP  is  encouraged  to  continue  the  survey,  and  once  the  the  majority  of  responses  completed, it will reveal if significant leakage occurs so then a recalculation of leakage would be necessary.  The Forward Action Request shall be considered during the “Performance Assessment”.   Note: this FAR is carried over from initial certification and GS review.)

Life on land.summary: The evidence provided does not directly measure the indicator variable 'Trends in species diversity' as it only mentions the number of tree species present in the forest ecosystem, which remains constant over time. The evidence does not provide explicit quantitative data on the proportion of forest area occupied by different tree species, the percentage of native species, or the percentage of forest area with low tree species diversity. Therefore, the evidence fails to satisfy the measurement requirement of the indicator.

to 2018.

hectare).

Grevillea robusta, Cedrela odorata, Acacia mearnsii.

framework of EcoMakala project since 2017.

Life on land.summary: The evidence from the 28+May+2020+Verification+Report_Congo+project+Forestry_Clean.pdf, Page: 33, states that only native tree species may be planted in riparian buffer zones and invasive species shall be removed. However, this information does not directly measure the distribution and traits of native tree species in terrestrial ecosystems. The evidence does not contain explicit quantitative data that directly measures or clearly supports the indicator. Therefore, the evidence is qualitative and does not satisfy the indicator's measurement requirement.

How do invasive species affect the distr.content: : 3   C Water resources  4.  On  both  sides  of  permanent  or  temporary  water  bodies  (lakes,  streams,  rivers,  wetlands,  etc.)  riparian  buffer  zones  of  15  meters  shall  be  implemented  on  each  site.  In  these  riparian  buffer  zones:  (a) only native tree species3 may be planted, AND  (b) invasive species4 shall be removed, AND  (c) all existing vegetation shall be kept, AND  (d) no  timber  harvesting  activities  shall  take  place,  AND  (e) no use of fertilizer or chemical pesticides.  onclusion: | plantation follows the  land  contours  by  designing  the  rip  lines  accordingly.  For  prevention  of  compacting,  the  soil  is  ripped  up  during  planting  and  also  sheep  grazing is done in a controlled way. Hence the project  fulfills the GS requirements as relevant to the criteria  for the areas under Performance certification.: This  requirement  is  not  relevant  since  this  project  area  is  managed  by  small  holders.  However  eucalyptus trees are planted no less than 20 m away  from  the  water  bodies.  The  verification  team  visited  one  plantation  and  found  the  same.  The  verification  team  has  reviewed  the  shapefiles/13/  and  based  on  the  onsite  visit,  has  identified  the  water  bodies.  The  verification  team  has  also  observed  that  there  is  no   tree species  growth  at  the  conservation  area.  All  the  vegetation  is  kept  as  it  is.  No  timber  activities  took  place.

Soil & water
In terms of soil, the forest plantations lead to stabilization of soils and 
fight against erosion, soil restauration in case of degraded soils and 
thus, protection of watershed.
Besides, the soil fertility is improved -

Date: 14/10/2018: ( Corresponding  to  the  FAR9  raised  by  GS  on  the  document  [File  FARs  Ecomakala_Jul11_final],  GS  stated  that Project areas are composed by agricultural and grazing/pasture lands, without forests at least 10 years  prior to project start date (20150928 Rapport_Detude_eligibilite_Ecomakala_V6 and shape files ‘Shapefiles –  Planting area’). Also in the mitigation measures, the proximity of the planting zones with PNVRi, risk elephant  and gorillas damages was not considered. Also displacement of grazing into the PNVRi or other wood land  should be assessed given the intensity of pastoralism in the project zone.   The response from PP stated that PP will ask each growers at the time of signing the contract amendment if  there has been or is expected in the future any shift of agricultural or livestock activities which might cause  leakage. If survey responses reveal that significant leakage occurs (contrary to what was expected ex-ante  as per template 5.6), leakage will be calculated following the guidance indicated on pages 34/35 of the A/R  requirements and be deducted in the first year (t=1). In regards to risks caused by animals from the PNVRi  (like elephants and gorillas), the same is minimal and not considered to be significant. All plantations have to  have a certain security margin to the park, i.e. have to be located at least 200 meters from the national park’s  borders. In a few areas of the park where some risk of migrating animals to outside of the park have been  identified, an electric fence has been built. It should be further noted that neither gorillas nor elephants feed  with any of the species included in the project. from carbon projects, the carbon project management was taken over by the provincial 
carbon management unit of North Kivu (UGP)5.
The carbon project management team is responsible for: 
- 
Identification of land eligible for reforestation and selection of landowners 
(growers) to be validated by UGP; 
- 
The production of viable seedlings for reforesting selected lands;  
- 
Ensure the distribution of seedlings to the participating farmers (growers);

E In  section  B1.1  of  the  monitoring  report,  responses  on  the  FARs  were  formulated.  The  same  responses have been added here.   coMakala  project  partners  are  prohibited  from  using  fertilizers  and  pesticides  in  reforestation  activities under the project without consulting WWF's advice. This was stressed during the periodical  awareness  raising  campaigns  (see  document  ’17.10.2018.Normes  techniques  de_plantation’,  ‘Formation_protection_plantations_  juillet2018’).  In  addition,  a  survey  carried  out  on    with  150  randomly selected planters further confirmed that none of the planters use synthetic fertilisers or  soil  amendments  on  their  plantations  (see  document  ‘20220927_Survey  with  planters_MP4’).  Documentation  provided  by  project  developer.  This  monitoring  period  fewer  trainings  than  anticipated took place. This can be explained by several reasons, including the Ebola outbreak in  2018-2020 in the North Kivu region , the global COVID-19 pandemic (2020 to 2022), both of which  involved measures including travel and gathering restrictions. This has affected the implementation  of the project and monitoring activities. The change of PP which included a transition period and  takeover of operations from WWF to UGP also posed some challenges on the follow-up. However,  now the new structure is fully completed and in place. The PP will ensure to continue inform planters  about the use of fertilizers and pesticides as well in future through awareness raising campaigns.: H   F VVB,  based  on  the  justification  and  supporting  documents,  confirms  that  EcoMakala  project  partners  are  prohibited  from  using  fertilizers  and  pesticides  in  reforestation  activities  under  the  project  without  consulting  WWF's  advice.  Further  through  the  assessment  of  “20220927_Survey  with  planters_MP4”,  VVB  confirms  that  a  comprehensive  survey  was  conducted,  involving  150  randomly  selected  planters.  The  survey  results  confirm  that  none  of  the  planters  reported  using  synthetic fertilizers or soil amendments on their plantations. PD assures that they will continue to  educate and inform planters about the importance of avoiding the use of fertilizers and pesticides.  ence, VVB confirms the EcoMakala project's commitment to sustainable practices by prohibiting  the use of fertilizers and pesticides in reforestation activities. The survey results further support the  adherence of planters to these guidelines, and PD's commitment to ongoing education ensures that  sustainable practices will continue to be upheld throughout the project's duration. Project boundary:  
 
The project area is located in the five territories Lubero, Rutshuru, Masisi, Nyiragongo 
and Beni in the province of North Kivu, around the Virunga National Park in Eastern 
Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC).
